title: Nature of point defects in bulk hexagonal diamond
authors: Ling Zhu, Xuanxuan Zhang, Guliqinayi Alimu, Chen-Min Dai, Chunlan Ma, Zenghua Cai
abstract: Hexagonal diamond (HD), an exotic carbon allotrope recently synthesized in bulk form, exhibits superior mechanical properties compared to cubic diamond (CD) and holds promise for advanced industrial and quantum applications. Using first-principles calcu-lations, we systematically investigate intrinsic defects, extrinsic dopants, and defect complexes in HD. Our study shows that VC dominates intrinsic conductivity, while Ci is unstable. Among extrinsic dopants, boron acts as a benign acceptor enhancing p-type conductivity, whereas nitrogen and phosphorus serve as effective donors for n-type conductivity. Group II and Group IV dopants, however, introduce high formation energies or neutral charge states with limited impact. Furthermore, VC, MgC and XV defect com-plexes display multiple spin and charge states within the HD band gap, highlighting their potential as color centers for hosting qubits. These results not only clarify the defect physics of HD but also demonstrate its broader implications for conductivity engineering and quantum technologies.
summary: This paper provides a comprehensive theoretical study of point defects in hexagonal diamond. It identifies key dopants for controlling electrical conductivity, specifically Boron for p-type and Nitrogen/Phosphorus for n-type. Crucially, it highlights several defect types that could serve as stable color centers for quantum computing. The work establishes a roadmap for engineering the electronic and quantum properties of this carbon allotrope.
main_problem: The study aims to systematically investigate the nature, stability, and electronic properties of intrinsic and extrinsic point defects and defect complexes in bulk hexagonal diamond (HD) for applications in conductivity engineering and quantum technologies.
main_result: The researchers identified Boron as an effective p-type acceptor and Nitrogen/Phosphorus as effective n-type donors, while also discovering that certain complexes like VC, MgC, and XV exhibit multiple spin and charge states suitable for use as color centers/qubits.
method: First-principles calculations using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the VASP code, employing the HSE06 hybrid functional and the FNV scheme for charge corrections.
model_or_system: The physical system is bulk hexagonal diamond (HD), an exotic carbon allotrope (lonsdaleite) with an ABAB stacking sequence, featuring both intrinsic (vacancies, interstitials) and extrinsic (Group II, III, IV, and V) defects.
key_observables: Defect formation energies, transition energy levels (TELs), charge states, spin polarization, and binding energies of defect complexes.
important_parameters: Chemical potentials under C-rich conditions, Fermi level position, and band gap width (calculated at 4-4.62 eV).
main_assumptions: Calculations were performed under C-rich conditions and assumed the stability of the HD phase without secondary phase formation.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows formation energies of various XV complexes; Figure 5 illustrates structural distortions in P-related complexes; Figure 6 displays calculated transition energy levels within the HD band gap.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the properties of HD, details the DFT computational methodology, presents a systematic investigation of intrinsic defects, moves to extrinsic dopants by group, analyzes defect complexes, and concludes with implications for quantum and electronic applications.
why_interesting: The identification of specific defect complexes (VC, MgC, XV) that host multiple spin and charge states within the band gap is highly relevant for the development of solid-state color centers for quantum information processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
